methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.rationale: Median across 3 same-sector + same-stage peers in Climate & Energy. Range: $110M - $200M.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in Climate & Bio-Manufacturing

private.name: Graphyte
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Series A), post-money $200M

private.name: Fourth Power
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Series A), post-money $150M

private.name: Steady Energy
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Series A), post-money $110M

data.narrative:
Carbyon, a Series A climate and energy company founded by Hans De Neve, is evaluated against a peer universe of 9 private companies and 1 public comparable (LNZA). With €22.3M raised to date, the valuation analysis relies on peer-median benchmarking across three same-sector, same-stage companies: Graphyte ($200M), Fourth Power ($150M), and Steady Energy ($110M).

The peer-median method yields a point estimate of $150M, anchoring the middle of the valuation range. The three-company sample spans $110M to $200M, establishing the outer bounds of reasonable valuations. Given the single-method reliance and limited peer sample size, the triangulated range is bounded at $128M (low) and $172M (high), with $150M as the median. This 35% spread reflects typical Series A dispersion.

Key valuation uncertainties stem from the narrow peer sample and lack of revenue or operational metrics in the provided data. The analysis is purely comparable-company driven and does not incorporate discounted cash flow or venture method frameworks. Investors should stress-test against the full Climate & Energy comp set (particularly TAE Technologies and Redwood Materials) and conduct sensitivity analysis on the peer valuations themselves, which may reflect market-specific timing or capital availability effects. A post-money valuation in the $128M–$172M range appears defensible on peer grounds, contingent on validation of Carbyon's technical differentiation and market traction relative to sampled comparables.
